section.title: The Residents of Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: The wall is finished, the Law has been read, and the covenant sealed, but the rebuilt city stands strangely empty. This movement records how Jerusalem was repopulated: the leaders already lived there, lots were cast to bring one in ten of the people into the holy city, and others willingly volunteered, with the whole community blessing them. Then comes the register of who settled there, the men of Judah and Benjamin, the priests, Levites, gatekeepers, temple servants, and officials, followed by the towns and villages the rest occupied. It is one movement: the holy city given its people.
section.thesis: A restored city is not walls alone but a people willing to dwell in it; so by lot and by glad volunteering, and under the blessing of the whole assembly, God fills His vulnerable holy city with named, counted people, a small foreshadowing of the day when the true holy city is filled with the redeemed.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the lots and the willing settlers, verses 1 to 2; the men of Judah and Benjamin in Jerusalem, verses 3 to 9; the priests, Levites, and gatekeepers, verses 10 to 19; the servants, overseers, and the king's agent, verses 20 to 24; and the towns and villages of Judah and Benjamin, verses 25 to 36.

units.label: Lots cast for the holy city
units.note: one in ten brought in, and the willing blessed

verses.text: Now the leaders of the people settled in Jerusalem, and the rest of the people cast {{goral|lots}} to bring one out of ten to live in the {{irhaqodesh|holy city}} of Jerusalem, while the remaining nine were to dwell in their own towns.
And the people blessed all the men who {{nadab|volunteered}} to live in Jerusalem.

units.label: The men of Judah and Benjamin in Jerusalem
units.note: heads of the provinces who settled in the city

verses.text: These are the heads of the provinces who settled in Jerusalem. (In the villages of Judah, however, each lived on his own property in their towns, the Israelites, priests, Levites, temple servants, and descendants of Solomon’s servants,

while some of the descendants of Judah and Benjamin settled in Jerusalem.) From the descendants of Judah: Athaiah son of Uzziah, the son of Zechariah, the son of Amariah, the son of Shephatiah, the son of Mahalalel, a descendant of Perez;
and Maaseiah son of Baruch, the son of Col-hozeh, the son of Hazaiah, the son of Adaiah, the son of Joiarib, the son of Zechariah, a descendant of Shelah.
The descendants of Perez who settled in Jerusalem totaled 468 men of valor.
From the descendants of Benjamin: Sallu son of Meshullam, the son of Joed, the son of Pedaiah, the son of Kolaiah, the son of Maaseiah, the son of Ithiel, the son of Jeshaiah;
and his followers Gabbai and Sallai, 928 men.
Joel son of Zichri was the officer over them, and Judah son of Hassenuah was over the Second District of the city.

units.label: The priests, Levites, and gatekeepers
units.note: those set apart for the service of the house of God

verses.text: From the priests: Jedaiah son of Joiarib; Jachin;
Seraiah son of Hilkiah, the son of Meshullam, the son of Zadok, the son of Meraioth, the son of Ahitub, the chief official of the house of God;
and their associates who did the work at the temple, 822 men; Adaiah son of Jeroham, the son of Pelaliah, the son of Amzi, the son of Zechariah, the son of Pashhur, the son of Malchijah;
and his associates, the leaders of families, 242 men; Amashai son of Azarel, the son of Ahzai, the son of Meshillemoth, the son of Immer;
and his associates, 128 mighty men of valor. Zabdiel son of Haggedolim was their overseer.
From the Levites: Shemaiah son of Hasshub, the son of Azrikam, the son of Hashabiah, the son of Bunni;
Shabbethai and Jozabad, two leaders of the Levites, who supervised the work outside the house of God;
Mattaniah son of Mica, the son of Zabdi, the son of Asaph, who led in thanksgiving and prayer; Bakbukiah, second among his associates; and Abda son of Shammua, the son of Galal, the son of Jeduthun.
The Levites in the holy city totaled 284.
And the gatekeepers: Akkub, Talmon, and their associates, who kept watch at the gates, 172 men.

units.label: Servants, overseers, and the king's agent
units.note: the rest of the people, and those who ordered the service

verses.text: The rest of the Israelites, with the priests and Levites, were in all the villages of Judah, each on his own inheritance.
The temple servants lived on the hill of Ophel, with Ziha and Gishpa over them.

Now the overseer of the Levites in Jerusalem was Uzzi son of Bani, the son of Hashabiah, the son of Mattaniah, the son of Mica. He was one of Asaph’s descendants, who were the singers in charge of the service of the house of God.
For there was a command from the king concerning the singers, an ordinance regulating their daily activities.
Pethahiah son of Meshezabel, a descendant of Zerah son of Judah, was the king’s agent in every matter concerning the people.

units.label: The towns and villages they occupied
units.note: from Beersheba to the Valley of Hinnom, and the towns of Benjamin

verses.text: As for the villages with their fields, some of the people of Judah lived in Kiriath-arba, Dibon, Jekabzeel, and their villages;
in Jeshua, Moladah, and Beth-pelet;
in Hazar-shual; in Beersheba and its villages;
in Ziklag; in Meconah and its villages;
in En-rimmon, Zorah, Jarmuth,
Zanoah, Adullam, and their villages; in Lachish and its fields; and in Azekah and its villages. So they settled from Beersheba all the way to the Valley of Hinnom.
The descendants of Benjamin from Geba lived in Michmash, Aija, and Bethel with its villages;
in Anathoth, Nob, Ananiah,
Hazor, Ramah, Gittaim,
Hadid, Zeboim, Neballat,
Lod, and Ono; and in the Valley of the Craftsmen.
And some divisions of the Levites of Judah settled in Benjamin.

goral.gloss: A lot, the object cast to reach a decision, and by extension the portion or share it assigns. The people cast lots to determine who would leave their own towns to live in Jerusalem. Scripture teaches that the lot's outcome is not chance but from the LORD, Proverbs 16:33, so the casting is an appeal to God's own choosing of who should fill His city.

irhaqodesh.gloss: The holy city, a title for Jerusalem as the place set apart by God's presence and His temple. The name recurs in verse 18. To live there was an honour and a burden: the city was holy because God dwelt there, yet still exposed and only half settled, so populating it was an act of devotion to the God whose house stood within its walls.

nadab.gloss: To offer or give willingly, of one's own free heart, the same root used of freewill offerings for the tabernacle and temple. Some came to Jerusalem by lot, but others volunteered, giving themselves freely to dwell in the vulnerable holy city; the people blessed them, honouring the glad, uncompelled sacrifice that true devotion to God so often requires.

basic.context: The wall is built, the Law has been read, and the people have sealed a covenant, but Jerusalem is still thinly populated, its houses not yet rebuilt. A city needs people, and living inside the holy city meant real cost: it was exposed, unfinished, and still surrounded by hostile neighbours. So the leaders, who already lived there, arranged for one in ten of the people to move in, chosen by lot, while others volunteered from the heart, and the whole community blessed them. The rest of the chapter is a register: who settled in Jerusalem, the men of Judah and Benjamin, the priests, Levites, gatekeepers, and temple servants, and which towns and villages the others occupied.
basic.hermeneutics: Much of this chapter is a list of names and places, an official record of how the holy city was repopulated. We do not read it for rules to copy but to see what God cares about: that His city has a people, that the burden of holding it is shared, and that willing sacrifice for God's dwelling is honoured. The lot was an ancient way of seeking God's decision, and the register quietly insists that these ordinary settlers, named one by one, mattered to God.
basic.originalLanguages: The people cast the goral, the lot, trusting that its outcome came from the LORD, to bring one in ten into the ir haqodesh, the holy city, Jerusalem set apart by God's presence. And beyond those chosen by lot, some nadab, volunteered, giving themselves freely, the same word used of freewill offerings, so that filling the holy city became a kind of living offering to God.
basic.typology: The holy city needing willing people to dwell in it, named and counted, points forward to the true holy city, the New Jerusalem, filled with the redeemed whose names are written in the Lamb's book of life. The one in ten drawn in by lot pictures God's sovereign choosing of a people to be His dwelling place.
basic.christ: This half empty holy city, needing people willing to bear its cost, looks ahead to Jesus, who by His own willing sacrifice secures and fills the true city of God. Where here a tenth were moved in by lot and a few volunteered, Christ gathers a countless multitude from every nation and makes them living stones in God's dwelling. The register of names foreshadows the day when God's people are known and counted, their names written in heaven.
basic.god: God cares that His city has a people. He is honoured when His people give themselves willingly to His purposes, even at cost, and He knows each of them by name. Behind the lot stands His own choosing, and behind the whole scene stands His steady work to establish a place where He will dwell with His people.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 16:33 says the lot's every decision is from the LORD. Psalm 87 sings of the glorious things spoken of the city of God. Hebrews 12:22 and Revelation 21 reveal the heavenly Jerusalem, the city this one only shadows.
basic.oneStory: A rebuilt Jerusalem was still empty until God gave it a people, some by lot, some by glad volunteering, all named and blessed. That vulnerable, half filled holy city points beyond itself to the true and lasting city of God, the New Jerusalem, which Christ fills with His redeemed, a people He has chosen, gathered, and knows each one by name.

context.literary: After the covenant of chapter ten, the narrative returns to a practical problem raised back in chapter seven: the city was large but its people few. Chapter eleven answers it with a brief scene of decision, verses 1 to 2, followed by a formal register of residents and settlements, closely paralleling the list in 1 Chronicles 9. The lists are not filler; they frame the repopulation as an ordered, God honouring act, and they set up the dedication of the wall in chapter twelve.
context.historical: In the fragile Persian period province of Yehud, Jerusalem was a religious and administrative centre but a hard place to live, still rebuilding, still watched by enemies. Moving into the city meant leaving inherited land and livelihood for exposure and expense, which is why lots were cast to distribute the burden fairly and why those who volunteered were publicly blessed. The mention of a royal command about the singers reflects Persian recognition of the temple's ordered worship.
context.canonical: The chapter belongs to the Bible's long concern with Jerusalem as the city of God, sung in the Psalms and longed for by the prophets. Its registers echo the tribal and priestly lists that run from Numbers through Chronicles, insisting that God keeps track of His people. And its half realised holy city stands among the many Old Testament shadows that reach their fulfilment in the heavenly Jerusalem of Hebrews and Revelation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record how the holy city was given its people after the wall was built, to honour those who bore the cost of living in it, and to show, through an ordered register of names and places, that the restored community and its worship were rightly established under God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a particular resettlement in post exilic Judah, by lot and by volunteering; it does not prescribe casting lots or a fixed tenth for us today. What endures is the value God places on a people for His city, the honour of willing sacrifice for His purposes, and the assurance that He knows and numbers those who are His.

debates.question: Why were lots cast to decide who would live in Jerusalem?

views.label: Seeking God's own choice
views.case: In Israel the lot was a recognised way of laying a decision before God, whose sovereign hand governed its outcome, Proverbs 16:33; casting lots for the holy city acknowledged that its population should be settled by God's appointment rather than mere human preference.

views.label: Fairly distributing a costly burden
views.case: Living in the exposed, unfinished city was a hardship few would choose, so the lot spread the obligation evenly across the towns, one household in ten, preventing favouritism and ensuring the burden fell impartially.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold together: the community sought God's decision and shared the cost justly, and the volunteers who came beyond the lot show that devotion, not only duty, filled the holy city.

debates.question: Why does Scripture devote so much space to a list of names and towns?

views.label: An official record of the restored community
views.case: The register functions as an archive, documenting the priestly, Levitical, and tribal makeup of the resettled city and its territory, securing the community's ordering and its lines of service and inheritance for later generations.

views.label: A roll of honour before God
views.case: By naming settlers, servants, and singers one by one, the text quietly declares that these ordinary people mattered to God; to be counted among the residents of the holy city was itself an honour, and the list bears witness that God knows His people by name.

debates.bearing: Whether read as archive or honour roll, the effect is the same: God's city is not an abstraction but a named people, which points ahead to the day when the redeemed are known and numbered in the city of God.

study.originalLanguages: Two words carry the weight of the opening scene. The people cast the goral, the lot, the object thrown to reach a decision; but Israel knew that the lot's outcome was no accident, for its every decision is from the LORD, so casting lots for the city was an appeal to God's own choosing of who should fill it. And the place they were chosen to fill is the ir haqodesh, the holy city, Jerusalem named holy not for its walls but for the presence of God whose house stood within it, a title so weighty that the text repeats it of the Levites gathered there. Then, beyond those the lot selected, some nadab, volunteered, gave themselves of a free and willing heart, the very word used of the freewill offerings brought for the tabernacle and temple; and the people blessed them. So the repopulation of Jerusalem is framed as an offering: a holy city, God's own choosing, and hearts that gave themselves gladly to dwell where He dwelt.

typology.type: The holy city given a willing, named people
typology.fulfillment: The New Jerusalem filled with the redeemed of every nation, whose names are written in the Lamb's book of life, Revelation 21:2-3 and 21:27.

typology.type: One in ten drawn into the city by lot
typology.fulfillment: God's sovereign choosing of a people to be His dwelling place, built together as living stones, Ephesians 1:4 and 1 Peter 2:4-5.

typology.type: The register of residents of the holy city
typology.fulfillment: The names written in heaven, the citizens of the city of God, Luke 10:20 and Philippians 3:20.

study.christ: A rebuilt city with too few people to fill it is a quiet picture of a need only Christ can finally meet. Here the holy city is populated by lot and by a handful of volunteers who accept its cost; there, at the true city of God, Jesus by His own willing sacrifice gathers a great multitude that no one can number and settles them forever in the presence of God. He is the one who makes His people living stones in God's dwelling, who welcomes outsiders as fellow citizens, and who writes their names in the Lamb's book of life so that none of the redeemed is lost or forgotten. Where these settlers gave themselves freely to a vulnerable earthly Jerusalem, Christ gave Himself freely to secure the Jerusalem that cannot be shaken, and He fills it with the very people He has bought.
study.god: The chapter shows a God who is not content with empty walls but wants His city inhabited, His house served, His presence surrounded by a people. He honours willing sacrifice, blessing through His people those who give themselves gladly to His purposes even at real cost. Behind the casting of the lot stands His sovereign choosing, and behind the long register stands His intimate knowledge: He counts and names those who are His. This is the God who is always working to establish a dwelling place where He and His people are together, a purpose that runs from the tabernacle to the temple to the New Jerusalem.

crossReferences.note: The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD, the conviction behind casting lots for the holy city.
Glorious things are spoken of the city of God, Zion, the holy city this chapter labours to fill.
Believers have come to the heavenly Jerusalem, the city of the living God, which the earthly city only shadows.
The New Jerusalem comes down from God, and His dwelling is with His people, the true and final holy city.

study.oneStory: The wall was rebuilt, but a city is its people, and Jerusalem still stood half empty until, by lot and by glad volunteering, God gave it named residents whom the whole community blessed. That vulnerable, only partly filled holy city points beyond itself to the lasting city of God. Christ, by His willing sacrifice, secures the New Jerusalem and fills it with a people He has chosen, gathered from every nation, and knows each one by name, a holy city that will never again stand empty.

quiz.q: How were the residents of Jerusalem chosen?

options.text: The leaders already lived there, one in ten of the rest were brought in by lot, and others volunteered.
options.note: Verses 1-2 describe the lot for a tenth of the people and those who freely volunteered.

options.text: Only priests and Levites were permitted to live in the city.
options.note: Priests and Levites did settle there, but so did men of Judah and Benjamin and others; it was not priests alone.

options.text: The Persian king ordered a forced relocation.
options.note: The king's command touched the singers' service, not a forced repopulation; the move came by lot and volunteering.

options.text: Everyone was required to leave their towns and move to Jerusalem.
options.note: Only one in ten were brought in; the remaining nine stayed in their own towns.

quiz.q: Why was living in Jerusalem treated as a costly, honourable thing?

options.text: The holy city was still exposed and unfinished, so those who dwelt there bore real risk and expense for God's dwelling place.
options.note: The city was vulnerable and thinly settled, which is why the burden was shared and the willing were blessed.

options.text: The city was the wealthiest place in the province.
options.note: Jerusalem's honour was as the holy city of God's presence, not as a place of riches; living there was a burden, not a prize.

options.text: Only the strongest soldiers were allowed inside.
options.note: Men of valour are named, but the residents included priests, Levites, servants, and families, not soldiers only.

options.text: Living there freed people from all obligations.
options.note: The opposite: it added the obligation and cost of holding the vulnerable holy city.

quiz.q: What did the people do for those who volunteered to live in Jerusalem?

options.text: They blessed them, honouring their willing sacrifice.
options.note: Verse 2 says the people blessed all who volunteered to live in Jerusalem.

options.text: They paid them a wage from the temple treasury.
options.note: The text records a blessing, not a payment; their reward was honour, not wages.

options.text: They exempted them from the covenant obligations.
options.note: No exemption is mentioned; the community blessed rather than excused them.

options.text: They resented them for leaving their farmland.
options.note: The people honoured, not resented, those who volunteered.

quiz.q: Why does the chapter list so many names of settlers, priests, and towns?

options.text: To record the restored community and to show that God knows and honours His people one by one.
options.note: The register orders the community and quietly declares that each named resident mattered to God.

options.text: To prove that only these families were truly saved.
options.note: The list records who settled where; it is not a roster of the saved.

options.text: To fill space with material of no real importance.
options.note: The naming is deliberate, testifying that God counts His people, not mere filler.

options.text: To rank the people from greatest to least.
options.note: The list orders residence and service, not a ranking of worth.

quiz.q: How does this half filled holy city point to Christ?

options.text: It looks ahead to the true holy city, the New Jerusalem, which Christ fills with the redeemed whose names are written in the Lamb's book of life.
options.note: The earthly city that needed people foreshadows the city of God that Christ secures and fills.

options.text: It teaches that God's city will always remain empty and incomplete.
options.note: The opposite: it points forward to the city God fully fills in Christ.

options.text: It shows that outsiders can never belong to God's city.
options.note: Christ gathers people from every nation into the city of God, welcoming outsiders.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ or the gospel.
options.note: The holy city and its people point directly to Christ and the New Jerusalem.

questions: Living in the holy city cost these settlers comfort and safety for the sake of God's dwelling place. Where is God calling you to a costly obedience that others might never notice?
Some came by lot and some volunteered freely, and the willing were blessed. What would it look like for you to give yourself gladly to God's purposes rather than only when required?
The community publicly blessed those who bore the burden of the city. Who in your church quietly carries hard, unseen loads, and how could you honour and encourage them this week?
God had every settler named and counted in His city. How does it steady you to know that God knows you by name and has written the names of His people in heaven?

This vulnerable, half filled city points to the New Jerusalem that Christ fills with His people. How does the certainty of that lasting city shape the way you hold the temporary things you are tempted to cling to?

translationNotes.note: The holy city renders ir haqodesh, a title for Jerusalem as the place of God's presence; the BSB footnote notes that the remaining nine follows the Hebrew idiom nine hands, meaning nine parts or tenths of the people.
Temple servants renders the Hebrew Nethinim, the given ones assigned to assist the Levites in the temple; the same term stands behind temple servants in verse 21.
The Second District renders a phrase the BSB footnote also gives as the Second Quarter, a newer section of Jerusalem, or as second in command of the city.
The Valley of the Craftsmen renders the Hebrew Ge-harashim, a place name preserving the memory of the artisans who once worked there.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Nehemiah 11:1-36. The chapter is largely a register of names and places; all 36 verses are included in order, assembled by concatenating the text segments of each verse and ignoring noteId, lineBreak, poem, and heading markers. Every em or en dash in the source (verses 3, 8, 12, 13, 14, 19) was converted to a comma or clean break; no dash characters were introduced. Hyphens within compound place names, such as Kiriath-arba and Beth-pelet, are ordinary hyphens and are retained.
